content: helpers.c is sort of a pain point for me when rebasing all the patches we have flying around. How do people feel about changing this to be a helpers/ directory with a wildcard rule for *.cfiles in there? Then we can put related functionality in its own file and we won't have to manually merge changes all the time.
It seems nobody is opposed to this :)
I agree with you, it's difficult to solve the merge conflicts in helpers.c

Splitting in files is a good idea, and it is how it will be done in the new build system. I prefer to focus on that though, instead of modifying what we have now, but please feel free to submit a patch for that.

I am not sure what you mean by "wildcard rule". If you mean the actual rule, that is fine, but the list of files to be built should still be listed explicitly.

Miguel Ojeda said:

I am not sure what you mean by "wildcard rule". If you mean the actual rule, that is fine, but the list of files to be built should still be listed explicitly.

I was considering something like this:

diff --git a/rust/Makefile b/rust/Makefile
index 9d2a16cc91cb..7ed0b3d3c5ba 100644
--- a/rust/Makefile
+++ b/rust/Makefile
@@ -8,7 +8,7 @@ always-$(CONFIG_RUST) += exports_core_generated.h

 # Missing prototypes are expected in the helpers since these are exported
 # for Rust only, thus there is no header nor prototypes.
-obj-$(CONFIG_RUST) += helpers.o
+obj-$(CONFIG_RUST) += helpers/*.o
 CFLAGS_REMOVE_helpers.o = -Wmissing-prototypes -Wmissing-declarations

 always-$(CONFIG_RUST) += libmacros.so

But in the light of recent backdoor in xz that might not be such a good idea :grimacing:
Yeah, I mean, it is not without precedent in the kernel, but it is unusual, and I would still avoid it unless we truly have too many files, i.e. I would keep it simple.
@Miguel Ojeda https://github.com/metaspace/linux/commit/b32e2aed529dfdb2329c11a15c426d901187753b what do you think, should I just send it?

A few things that come to mind:

RUST_HELPERS = \

Can be :=.

rust/helpers/README

We can use Markdown for this (like in the alloc one).

Also, it would need some rewriting, since the comment "this file" does not make sense anymore if it is in the README etc.

Or if you are keeping helpers.c for some reason, you could simply keep it as a C comment (but I guess that does not make too much sense either -- do we need that empty one, though?).

rust/helpers/build_assert.c

Hmm... in general when I see something like this I would recommend keeping the asserts wherever they make sense (rather than putting all asserts in a single place), but I guess in this case there is no good place to put that one and we may not have other build asserts often, so I guess it is OK.

Though, if we keep the helpers.c one that is empty now, should we put it there? After all, it is a general build assert on the approach.

rust/helpers/Makefile

Since we are not putting the obj-* list in this file (which is what I would have expected if I see that file in the folder), I would just get rid of this -- that way you can also avoid exporting the variable and the recursion into the folder (make it lowercase the name then, please).

Or was there a reason you did it like this?

I will need to review the cmd_cat too -- bindgen does not support several headers, right?

Miguel Ojeda said:

Or if you are keeping helpers.c for some reason, you could simply keep it as a C comment (but I guess that does not make too much sense either -- do we need that empty one, though?).

No, that is a mistake.

Miguel Ojeda said:

rust/helpers/README

We can use Markdown for this (like in the alloc one).

It _is_ markdown?
I meant the extension + using the usual titles we do (# Header).

Miguel Ojeda said:

Since we are not putting the obj-* list in this file (which is what I would have expected if I see that file in the folder), I would just get rid of this -- that way you can also avoid exporting the variable and the recursion into the folder (make it lowercase the name then, please).

I _did_ put the obj-* list in the makefile in the subdirectory?

Miguel Ojeda said:

Or was there a reason you did it like this?

kbuild documentation suggest this is the way.

Miguel Ojeda said:

I will need to review the cmd_cat too -- bindgen does not support several headers, right?

It does not, sadly. So this was to not generate one rust file for each helper C file. It seems to work fine like this. It just concatenates all the helper C files.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Miguel Ojeda said:

Or was there a reason you did it like this?

kbuild documentation suggest this is the way.

I can move it back into rust/Makefile no problem. It was just to conform to whatever guidelines already present.

I _did_ put the obj-* list in the makefile in the subdirectory?

The variable is there, but the list itself is not. i.e. what I mean is that in order to know what is getting done, you have to anyway go into the parent.

Miguel Ojeda said:

I _did_ put the obj-* list in the makefile in the subdirectory?

The variable is there, but the list itself is not. i.e. what I mean is that in order to know what is getting done, you have to anyway go into the parent.

Right, I get your point now.

Andreas Hindborg said:

kbuild documentation suggest this is the way.

Yeah, though it is a bit of an odd case, because we are also doing other things with the files from the parent. It is fine either way, I was just trying to simplify the changes a bit (i.e. so that you had to move less things around) and so that developers did not look into the file just to jump into the other one.
I guess doing it like you did can be argued to be "simpler" too, in that the C side of things looks "more normal" :)

I pushed some changes:

724790dea540 fix: Fix a bug en dependency calculation of combined helper file
b067991e99eb fix: Add a note about definition site for RUST_HELPERS
f5d953cab27f fix: Use markdown file format for readme and fix wording
34fbb526fcef fix: Use simple expansion assignment operator for defining list
b32e2aed529d kbuild: rust: split up helpers.c
• d18c9338446c fix: Remove empty helpers.c file
I have left some comments directly in each commit. Not sure if that leaves notifications, though. I hope that helps!
